original_abstract: Political conflict is fundamentally rooted in language, yet the sheer volume of written and spoken communication has historically posed a significant barrier to systematic analysis. While scholars have long recognized the importance of text, the labor-intensive nature of manual coding limited the scope of political science research. The emergence of automated text analysis offers a transformative solution by drastically lowering the costs associated with processing large-scale datasets. In this article, we provide a comprehensive guide to this evolving field, demonstrating how these methods have already begun to fulfill their potential in empirical research. However, we also caution against viewing automation as a panacea; these tools are not a replacement for rigorous theory or close reading, and they demand extensive, problem-specific validation. By surveying a broad spectrum of current methodologies, we offer practical guidance on validating model outputs and clarify common misconceptions within the existing literature. Ultimately, we argue that for automated text analysis to mature into a standard disciplinary tool, methodologists must continue to develop both innovative models and more robust framework for validation.
humanized_abstract: Politics is fundamentally a communicative act. However, due to the massive quantity of political speech and in particular political writing, analysis of this speech has generally been thought to be beyond the capacity of any reasonable research effort. Because political science as a discipline has long acknowledged the importance of political text, there have been few attempts to develop politically relevant data for analysis because to do so required the expenditure of huge resources of time and money. High scale computational costs have been so high that relatively few attempts have been made to collect the large bodies of data that would be required for serious scientific development of the method. Finally, the advent of cheaper and easier high scale computational text analysis tools and services means that political science based on text analysis is feasible at any number of scales and many techniques have now been proven to have at least some value. Nevertheless, use of any new tool as a serious substitute for human effort, analysis, or insight is contingent on that tool being more than trivial to use, less expensive than hand-driven alternatives, and on thorough knowledge of limitations and problems with such use of new technologies. Here, our goal is to introduce political science and a new generation of new quantitative text analyses. We also explain practices for validating findings that they yield; we also discuss a little of what is misleading in the literature associated with these methods and illustrate possible procedures with data from a project the first author has been leading on partisan media. Finally, for automation of these methods to become the routine tools they might be, the techniques and associated validation procedures have to be refined and a common political science vocabulary regarding use of the techniques in place of more traditional, “better,” human methods of qualitative data analysis will have to be established and agreed upon.
